title: Water Competence & Swimming
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Instruction] Access to a pool or safe swimming environment
[Instruction] A competent swimming instructor or mentor

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: swim instruction channels (Effortless Swimming, MySwimPro)

competence_markers.description: The student is comfortable with face fully submerged, eyes open (with goggles), and can blow bubbles underwater without distress
The student can back float for 2+ minutes without assistance (5 minutes is the full target)
The student can tread water in the deep end for 2+ minutes without touching the wall or bottom
The student can swim 25 yards in freestyle without stopping
The student can swim 25 yards in backstroke without stopping
The student can jump into deep water, surface, and swim to the nearest edge unassisted
The student can dive to the bottom of the shallow end (4-5 feet) and return to the surface
The student can articulate the water safety rules and demonstrates them consistently (no running, no swimming alone, float first if you fall in)
The student knows what to do if they see someone drowning (throw, reach, call — do not jump in)

cross_connections.description: General movement competence supports water competence. Body awareness, breath control, and comfort with physical challenge all transfer.
Water procurement and purification are survival skills; water competence is the physical survival skill — the ability to survive in water, not just use it.

Overcoming fear of deep water, learning to relax when your body wants to panic — these are direct IC3 applications. The student who learns to float in water over their head has practiced exactly the composure that IC3 develops.
Water awareness — recognizing currents, judging depth, assessing risks — is situational awareness applied to an aquatic environment.

content.why_this_matters:
Drowning is one of the leading causes of death for children your age. Not because water is evil or unusually dangerous, but because most people never learn to be truly comfortable and competent in water. They panic when their feet cannot touch the bottom. They exhaust themselves fighting the water instead of working with it. They never learn to float, which is the single skill that can save your life in almost any water emergency.

You are going to learn to swim — not as a hobby or a summer activity, but as a survival skill. When this packet is complete, you will be able to enter water over your head, remain calm, float without effort, swim to safety, and tread water long enough for help to arrive. These are not athletic achievements. They are the baseline for any person who might ever be near water — which is every person alive.

Water competence also builds physical qualities that land-based training cannot: breath control, comfort with an unfamiliar environment, full-body coordination, and the ability to manage fear in a situation where panic is the real danger. A person who is competent in water carries a calm confidence that extends far beyond the pool.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Comfort in water** — the ability to be in water over your head without panic
2. **Floating** — the survival skill that keeps you alive when you cannot swim to safety
3. **Treading water** — sustaining your head above water in deep water for extended periods
4. **Basic swimming strokes** — freestyle and backstroke with functional technique
5. **Breath control** — comfortable exhaling underwater and timing breaths with strokes
6. **Water entry and exit** — jumping into deep water, diving to the bottom, climbing out
7. **Basic water safety knowledge** — what to do if you fall in, how to help someone without entering the water, when to call for help
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Water Is Not the Enemy — Fear Is

The first thing to understand about water is that your body naturally floats. Not sinks — floats. If you fill your lungs with air and relax, your body will float at or near the surface. The reason people drown is not that water pulls them down. It is that panic makes them fight the water — thrashing, gasping, exhausting themselves — until they cannot keep their head above the surface.

The single most important skill in water is the ability to remain calm. A calm person floats. A calm person conserves energy. A calm person can think about what to do next. A panicked person does none of these things and makes every situation worse.

Everything in this packet is designed to build that calm through competence. You will not be thrown into deep water. You will build skills progressively, starting in water where you can stand, and moving to deeper water only when you are ready. By the time you are in water over your head, you will have the skills to handle it.

### Part 2: Getting Comfortable — The Foundation

Before any swimming technique, you need to be comfortable in water. This means:

**Face in the water.** Many children are afraid to put their face in. This is normal and this is where you start. Practice in the bathtub or a shallow pool: put your face in, blow bubbles out of your nose, open your eyes (with goggles). Do this until it is boring, not scary.

**Going underwater.** Submerge completely. Hold your breath. Open your eyes. Pick up objects from the bottom of the shallow end. The key is relaxation — hold your breath gently, do not gulp and clench.

**Back floating.** Lie on your back in shallow water with your mentor supporting your back. Fill your lungs. Relax. Let the water hold you. Gradually, your mentor reduces support until you are floating on your own. This skill — floating on your back — is the survival foundation. A person who can back float can survive in water almost indefinitely.

**Treading position.** In water where you can stand, practice the treading motion: legs doing a bicycle or eggbeater kick, arms sculling at the surface. Feel how the motion keeps you upright. You are not fighting the water — you are gently pushing it down and it pushes you up.

### Part 3: Floating — The Survival Skill

If you fall into water unexpectedly and cannot get out, floating keeps you alive.

**Back float technique:**
1. Fill your lungs with air — your lungs are your floatation device
2. Lean back until your ears are in the water
3. Spread your arms out to the sides
4. Let your hips rise — do not sit in the water, lie in it
5. Relax. The more tense you are, the more you sink. The more relaxed you are, the more you float.
6. Breathe normally — when you exhale, you will sink slightly; when you inhale, you will rise. This is normal. Do not panic during the exhale-sink phase.

**Practice:** Float on your back for progressively longer periods. Start with 30 seconds. Build to 2 minutes. Build to 5 minutes. When you can float for 5 minutes without effort, you have the survival foundation.

### Part 4: Treading Water

Treading water keeps your head above the surface while you are vertical in deep water. It is more tiring than floating but more practical in many situations — choppy water, cold water, water where you need to see what is around you.

**Eggbeater kick:** Your legs make alternating circular motions — like pedaling a bicycle, but wider and more circular. One leg pushes down while the other recovers. This is the most efficient treading kick because it provides constant upward force.

**Sculling arms:** Your hands move back and forth just below the surface, angled slightly downward. Think of spreading peanut butter on a table — smooth, continuous, side-to-side motion that pushes water down.

**Practice goal:** Tread water for 2 minutes continuously. Then 5 minutes. A person who can tread water for 5 minutes can survive most accidental water entries long enough for rescue.

### Part 5: Basic Strokes — Moving Through Water

**Freestyle (front crawl):**
- Body position: flat and horizontal, face in the water, hips near the surface
- Arms: reach forward, hand enters pinky-side first, pull through the water past your hip, recover above the water
- Legs: flutter kick — straight legs, small kicks from the hip, not the knee
- Breathing: turn your head to the side (do not lift it) to breathe; exhale while your face is in the water

**Backstroke:**
- Body position: flat on your back, ears in the water, hips up
- Arms: alternating overhead reach and pull — arm reaches behind you, enters the water pinky-first, pulls down past your hip
- Legs: flutter kick — same as freestyle but on your back
- Breathing: your face is always above water, so breathe normally

**The priority at this tier is functional swimming, not competitive technique.** The student needs to be able to swim 25 yards (one pool length) in freestyle and backstroke without stopping. Technique will be refined in Weathering Storms. At Stretching Branches, the standard is: can you get from here to there in the water reliably?

### Part 6: Water Entry

**Jumping in:** Start from the pool edge into deep water. Step off, do not dive. Enter feet-first. When comfortable, jump in with more height and confidence. The student should be comfortable jumping into water over their head and returning to the surface without assistance.

**Diving to the bottom:** In the shallow end (4-5 feet), swim down to the bottom, touch it, and return to the surface. This builds breath-hold capacity and comfort with being fully submerged and away from the surface.

### Part 7: Water Safety Rules

These are non-negotiable. They apply every time you are near water.

1. **Never swim alone.** Always have a buddy or a supervising adult.
2. **No running on pool decks or docks.** Wet surfaces are slip hazards. A fall onto concrete or wood next to water can be more dangerous than the water itself.
3. **If you fall in unexpectedly: float first, then plan.** Do not thrash. Roll onto your back. Float. Catch your breath. Then decide: can you swim to the edge? Is someone nearby? Call for help calmly.
4. **If you see someone drowning: do not jump in.** Throw them something that floats (a life ring, a kickboard, a cooler). Extend something they can grab (a pole, a towel, a branch). Call for adult help immediately. A drowning person will instinctively grab and push down anyone who tries to save them — even trained rescuers struggle with this.
5. **Respect currents, waves, and open water.** Pools are controlled environments. Lakes, rivers, and oceans are not. Open water is addressed in Weathering Storms, but the basic principle applies now: water that moves has power that you cannot match. Do not fight currents — swim across them.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Water Comfort
**Developing well:** Face in water without distress. Comfortable fully submerged. Opens eyes underwater with goggles. Retrieves objects from the bottom. No signs of panic or breath-holding tension.

**Developing slowly:** Resists putting face in water. Tenses and gasps when submerged. Keeps eyes squeezed shut underwater. Clutches the wall or your arm in water deeper than waist height.

**Diagnostic questions:** How much water exposure has the student had? Fear of water often correlates with insufficient exposure rather than innate phobia. Increase frequency of water sessions (even bathtub face-in-water practice) before concluding there is a problem.

### 2. Back Float
**Developing well:** Floats on back for 2+ minutes with relaxed body position. Hips near the surface. Breathing is normal. Can hold a conversation while floating.

**Developing slowly:** Sinks immediately. Hips drop. Body is tense and rigid. Cannot relax enough to let the water support them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student exhaling fully? Full lungs float; empty lungs sink. Is their head position correct? Looking straight up with ears in the water is key — lifting the head drops the hips. Are you supporting them long enough during the learning phase? Some children need weeks of supported floating before they can do it independently.

### 3. Treading Water
**Developing well:** Treads in deep water for 2+ minutes with consistent kick and sculling motion. Head stays above water. Can maintain a conversation. Not exhausted at the end.

**Developing slowly:** Sinks after 15–30 seconds. Arms and legs move frantically rather than rhythmically. Head bobs below the surface repeatedly.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been taught the eggbeater kick specifically? Instinctive treading is inefficient. The eggbeater kick provides constant upward force and is dramatically more sustainable. If the student has only been doing a bicycle kick, teach the eggbeater.

### 4. Swimming Strokes
**Developing well:** Swims 25 yards in freestyle with face in water, rhythmic breathing to the side, and consistent kick. Swims 25 yards in backstroke with ears in water and steady kick. Not perfect form — functional form. They get across the pool reliably.

**Developing slowly:** Cannot swim 25 yards without stopping. Head-up freestyle (face never goes in the water). No breathing rhythm. Exhausted after half a length.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough pool time? Swimming technique requires volume — you cannot learn to swim in one session per week. Twice weekly is minimum for meaningful progress. Is the student practicing breathing technique separately from swimming? Side-breathing in freestyle is its own skill and should be drilled at the wall before incorporating it into full swimming.

### 5. Water Safety Knowledge
**Developing well:** Can articulate the rules without prompting. Demonstrates them consistently — never runs on the deck, never swims without supervision, articulates the "throw, reach, call" rescue sequence.

**Developing slowly:** Knows the rules when quizzed but does not follow them consistently. Runs on the deck when excited. Would jump in to "save" someone without thinking.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you enforcing the rules every time? If running on the deck is sometimes ignored, it is not a rule — it is a suggestion. Consistency of enforcement determines consistency of compliance.
